preFix.title: Fitting a curtain pole
preFix.subtitle: Mark, drill, and fix a standard two-bracket curtain pole above a window
preFix.excerpt: How to fit a curtain pole with two or more brackets. Covers positioning above the window, getting both brackets level, the correct fixing method for masonry and plasterboard, and sliding the rings and pole into position.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Traditional domestic fixing practice.

content.text: A curtain pole looks better and hangs better when it is positioned with thought rather than at whatever height is convenient for drilling. The standard height is 150 to 200 mm above the window top (this hides the blind or shutter mechanism above). The standard width is 150 to 300 mm beyond each side of the window frame, enough that the open curtain clears the glass.

Tap the wall. A solid thud suggests brick or block, drill with the standard masonry bit in a hammer-action cordless drill, use a wall plug, and screw the bracket. A hollow sound means a plasterboard partition: use a stud finder or a small drill bit to locate the timber studs, or use a
rated for the bracket load (curtain poles with heavy fabric can exert a surprising load on the fixing).

Use a tape measure and pencil to mark the intended position for each bracket. For a two-bracket pole, place the outer brackets 100 to 150 mm in from the pole ends. Long poles (over 1800 mm) need a central support bracket. Mark the centre of each bracket fixing hole on the wall, using the spirit level to confirm that all marks are at the same height before drilling a single hole.
Step 3, Drill and fix the first bracket

Check the drill bit size matches the wall plug diameter (usually 6 mm for a 6 mm plug). Drill to the depth of the plug, mark the depth on the bit with a piece of tape as a guide. Insert the plug flush with the wall surface. Hold the bracket in position, insert the screw, and drive until the bracket is firm but do not over-tighten yet.
Step 4, Level and fix the second bracket

Hold the spirit level across the top of the fixed bracket and the pencil mark for the second bracket. Adjust the mark vertically until the bubble is centred. Drill the second bracket position and fix as above. Once both brackets are fixed, lay the spirit level across the bracket tops to confirm they are level, then fully tighten all screws. If a bracket has two fixing holes, use both: a single screw under a loaded curtain pole will rotate and drop eventually.
Step 5, Thread the rings and hang the pole

Thread the curtain rings onto the pole before placing it in the brackets. Many poles have finials (decorative end caps) that unscrew, remove them to thread the rings. Count the rings: aim for 2 to 3 rings per 30 cm of curtain width. Sit the pole in the brackets, click or tighten the bracket closures, and replace the finials. Check the pole for wobble; tighten any bracket screws that are loose.
Deep window reveal: use a bracket with a longer
(75 to 100 mm) so the curtain hangs clear of the window ledge.
Bay window: bay poles are jointed at the corners with angled connectors. Fix one bracket on each bay face, angled to match the wall angle, and connect the pole sections at the joint.

items.fix: Re-drill a larger hole, fit a hollow-wall anchor rated for the load, or locate the timber stud and screw directly into it.
items.cause: Wall plug used in a hollow cavity without a cavity fixing, or single screw under a two-hole bracket
items.symptom: Bracket pulls out of wall under the curtain weight

items.fix: Refit the lower bracket. Pack behind the bracket face with a small timber shim to raise it without re-drilling.
items.cause: Bracket marks not checked with a level before drilling
items.symptom: Pole is not level after fitting

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
brand-trademark: brand name "Anchor" — generic equivalent is "salted butter"
